name: Logical Reasoning Under Constraints
instructions: This section tests your ability to reason through conditional rules, constraints, and multi-step logic. Some questions are quick deductions with a visible timer — trust your reasoning and commit. Others are deeper problems with more time. For written questions, briefly explain your reasoning. You cannot go back to previous questions.

questions.prompt: A company policy states: 'All expenses over $200 require manager approval.' Jamie submitted a $180 expense without manager approval. Which statement is true?

options.text: Jamie should have gotten approval to be safe
Not enough information to determine if Jamie followed the policy

questions.explanation: The policy requires approval for expenses OVER $200. $180 is not over $200, so no approval is required. Jamie followed the policy as stated. Option (c) is practical advice, not a logical evaluation of the policy. Option (d) is wrong because the policy is clearly stated.

questions.prompt: If the following is true: 'Every project that is behind schedule gets a status review,' and Project X did NOT get a status review — what can you conclude?

options.text: Project X is not behind schedule

questions.explanation: This is contrapositive reasoning: if behind schedule → status review. No status review → not behind schedule. We can only conclude it's NOT behind schedule — we can't say it's 'on schedule' because it could be ahead of schedule or in some other state. Option (b) is the precise logical conclusion.

questions.prompt: A manager argues: 'We hired 10 new people last quarter and revenue went up. Therefore, hiring more people increases revenue.' What is the flaw in this reasoning?

options.text: 10 people is too small a sample to draw conclusions
Correlation does not prove causation — revenue may have increased for other reasons
Revenue always goes up over time due to inflation
The manager should have compared revenue per employee

questions.explanation: The reasoning assumes that because two things happened together (hiring + revenue increase), one caused the other. Revenue could have risen due to seasonal trends, a new product, market conditions, or many other factors. This is a classic post hoc ergo propter hoc fallacy.

questions.prompt: Rule: 'Documents marked Confidential can only be shared with employees who have signed an NDA.' Lee has signed an NDA. Can Lee access a Confidential document?

options.text: No — signing an NDA is necessary but may not be sufficient
Yes — anyone with an NDA automatically gets access

questions.explanation: The rule says Confidential documents CAN ONLY be shared with NDA-signed employees — meaning NDA is a necessary condition. But the rule doesn't say NDA is sufficient on its own. There might be additional requirements (need-to-know, role-based access, manager approval). We cannot conclude that Lee CAN access the document — only that Lee hasn't been excluded by this particular rule.

questions.prompt: Three teams submitted proposals. The rules state: 'The proposal with the lowest cost wins, unless it scores below 70 on quality, in which case it is disqualified.' Team A: $50K, quality 65. Team B: $80K, quality 90. Team C: $60K, quality 75. Who wins?

options.text: Team C — lowest cost among qualified proposals
No winner can be determined from this information

questions.explanation: Team A is disqualified (quality 65 < 70). Among remaining qualified proposals, Team C ($60K, quality 75) has a lower cost than Team B ($80K, quality 90). The rule selects lowest cost among qualified entries.

questions.prompt: Given the following rules for a training program, which employee is eligible to attend?
questions.context:
Rules:
1. Employees must have been with the company for at least 1 year.
2. Employees must have completed the online prerequisite course.
3. Managers can nominate employees who haven't completed the prerequisite, but only if the employee has 3+ years of experience.
4. Contractors are not eligible regardless of tenure.

Employees:
- Dana: 2 years tenure, completed prerequisite, full-time employee
- Eli: 4 years tenure, did NOT complete prerequisite, full-time employee, nominated by manager
- Fran: 6 months tenure, completed prerequisite, full-time employee
- Glen: 3 years tenure, completed prerequisite, contractor
questions.explanation: Dana: 2 years (✓ rule 1), prerequisite done (✓ rule 2), full-time (✓ rule 4) → ELIGIBLE. Eli: 4 years (✓ rule 1), no prerequisite but 3+ years + manager nomination (✓ rule 3), full-time (✓ rule 4) → ELIGIBLE. Fran: 6 months (✗ rule 1) → NOT ELIGIBLE. Glen: contractor (✗ rule 4) → NOT ELIGIBLE regardless of other qualifications.

questions.prompt: A company is assigning 4 employees (Kim, Leo, Mia, Noor) to 4 different projects (P1, P2, P3, P4). Given the constraints below, which assignment is valid?
questions.context:
Constraints:
1. Kim cannot work on P2 or P4.
2. Leo must work on either P1 or P3.
3. If Mia works on P2, then Noor must work on P3.
4. Each person works on exactly one project, and each project has exactly one person.
questions.explanation: Option (a): Mia→P2 triggers constraint 3 (Noor must be on P3), but Noor is on P4. VIOLATES constraint 3. Option (b): Mia→P2 triggers constraint 3 (Noor must be on P3), but Noor is on P4. VIOLATES constraint 3. Option (c): Kim→P1 (not P2/P4 ✓), Leo→P3 (P1 or P3 ✓), Mia→P4 (not P2, so constraint 3 doesn't trigger ✓). ALL CONSTRAINTS MET. Option (d): Kim→P4. VIOLATES constraint 1 (Kim cannot work on P2 or P4).

questions.prompt: Based on the following statements, what can you conclude?
questions.context:
Statements:
1. All orders placed before noon are shipped the same day.
2. Orders requiring custom packaging are never shipped the same day.
3. Order #445 was placed at 10:30 AM and requires custom packaging.

options.text: Order #445 will be shipped the same day because it was placed before noon
Order #445 will NOT be shipped the same day because of custom packaging
The statements contain a contradiction — the rules conflict for Order #445
Not enough information to determine when Order #445 ships

questions.explanation: Statement 1 says Order #445 MUST ship same day (placed before noon). Statement 2 says it CANNOT ship same day (custom packaging). Both rules apply to Order #445 and they produce contradictory requirements. The correct observation is that the rules as stated are internally inconsistent for this case. This tests the ability to recognize logical contradictions rather than trying to resolve them by picking one rule over another.

questions.prompt: A committee must select exactly 3 members from a group of 6 candidates. Given the constraints, which team is valid?
questions.context:
Candidates: Ava, Ben, Cal, Dee, Eve, Fin

Constraints:
1. If Ava is selected, Ben must also be selected.
2. If Cal is selected, Dee cannot be selected.
3. Eve and Fin cannot both be selected.
4. Ben and Cal cannot both be selected.
questions.explanation: Option (a): Ava selected → Ben must be selected (✓ rule 1). Cal not selected (rule 2 doesn't apply). Eve selected, Fin not (✓ rule 3). Ben and Cal not both selected (✓ rule 4). ALL CONSTRAINTS MET. Option (b): Ben and Cal both selected (✗ rule 4). Option (c): Ava selected but Ben not selected (✗ rule 1). Option (d): Cal and Dee both selected (✗ rule 2).

questions.prompt: A team lead says: 'None of our delayed projects had a risk assessment done upfront. So if we do risk assessments, our projects won't be delayed.' Is this reasoning valid?

options.text: Yes — the evidence shows risk assessments prevent delays
No — the absence of risk assessments in delayed projects doesn't prove they would have prevented delays
Yes — it's a reasonable precaution based on the data
No — risk assessments cause delays because they take time

questions.explanation: This is the fallacy of denying the antecedent / affirming the consequent in reverse. Just because all delayed projects lacked risk assessments doesn't mean risk assessments would have prevented the delays. There could be other causes. Also, projects WITH risk assessments might also get delayed — we have no data on those. Option (c) is a pragmatic argument, not a logical evaluation. Option (d) introduces an unrelated claim.

questions.prompt: A warehouse processes orders in priority order. Given the rules and the current queue, which order ships next?
questions.context:
Priority rules (applied in order — first rule that differentiates wins):
1. Express orders ship before standard orders.
2. Among same-speed orders, orders with perishable items ship first.
3. Among orders still tied, the earliest order placed ships first.

Current queue:
- Order A: Standard, non-perishable, placed 9:00 AM
- Order B: Express, non-perishable, placed 10:30 AM
- Order C: Standard, perishable, placed 8:15 AM
- Order D: Express, perishable, placed 11:00 AM
- Order E: Express, non-perishable, placed 9:45 AM
questions.explanation: Step 1: Express orders first → B, D, E are express. A and C are eliminated. Step 2: Among B, D, E — perishable first → D is perishable. B and E are not. D ships next. The full priority order would be: D, E (earliest express non-perishable), B, C (perishable standard), A.

questions.prompt: Read the argument below. In 2–4 sentences, explain whether the conclusion follows logically from the evidence — and if not, identify the flaw.
questions.context: Argument: 'Our customer complaints dropped by 40% after we redesigned the support page on our website. This proves the new design is better at resolving customer issues.'

constraints.placeholder_text: State whether the conclusion is logically valid and explain why or why not...

criteria: Identifies that correlation (redesign + fewer complaints) doesn't prove causation
Suggests alternative explanations (fewer customers, harder to find complaint form, seasonal variation, other changes happened simultaneously)
Distinguishes between 'fewer complaints' and 'better issue resolution' — complaints could drop for reasons unrelated to resolution quality
Notes the possibility that the redesign made it harder to complain, not easier to resolve issues

levels.label: Exceptional
levels.description: Comprehensive, well-structured, identifies non-obvious issues/steps. Shows systems thinking.

levels.label: Strong
levels.description: Covers key points with good structure. May miss one subtle element but demonstrates clear reasoning.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Identifies obvious issues/steps but lacks depth or misses important considerations.

levels.label: Weak
levels.description: Superficial or partially correct. Shows some reasoning but significant gaps.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Misunderstands the task, provides irrelevant response, or demonstrates no structured thinking.

questions.prompt: Read the scenario and the proposed conclusion. In 2–4 sentences, explain whether the conclusion is justified — and what additional information you would need to be confident in it.
questions.context:
Scenario: A company tested two versions of its checkout page. Version A (the original) had a 3.2% conversion rate over 4 weeks with 50,000 visitors. Version B (the new design) had a 3.8% conversion rate over 1 week with 5,000 visitors.

Conclusion: 'Version B is better. We should switch to it immediately.'

constraints.placeholder_text: Evaluate the conclusion and explain what's missing...

criteria: Identifies the sample size disparity (50K vs 5K) as a concern for statistical significance
Notes the time period difference (4 weeks vs 1 week) — weekly fluctuations, day-of-week effects, or promotions could skew results
Questions whether the difference (3.2% vs 3.8%) is statistically significant or within normal variation
Suggests running Version B for a longer period or with a larger sample before concluding
Considers whether other variables changed between the two periods (marketing campaigns, seasonality, traffic source mix)

levels.label: Exceptional
levels.description: Comprehensive, well-structured, identifies non-obvious issues/steps. Shows systems thinking.

levels.label: Strong
levels.description: Covers key points with good structure. May miss one subtle element but demonstrates clear reasoning.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Identifies obvious issues/steps but lacks depth or misses important considerations.

levels.label: Weak
levels.description: Superficial or partially correct. Shows some reasoning but significant gaps.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Misunderstands the task, provides irrelevant response, or demonstrates no structured thinking.

questions.prompt: Read the policy and the situation below. In 2–4 sentences, explain whether the action taken was correct according to the rules, and identify any ambiguity in the policy that makes this hard to determine.
questions.context:
Policy: 'Employees who work more than 8 hours in a single day are entitled to overtime pay at 1.5x their hourly rate. Overtime must be pre-approved by a supervisor.'

Situation: Alex worked 10 hours on Tuesday to meet a client deadline. Alex's supervisor was out sick and unreachable. Alex did not get pre-approval. Alex's manager denied the overtime pay, citing lack of pre-approval.

constraints.placeholder_text: Evaluate the situation against the policy and identify any ambiguity...

criteria: Identifies the tension between the entitlement ('are entitled to') and the pre-approval requirement — these can conflict
Notes the ambiguity: is pre-approval a condition for receiving pay, or a procedural expectation that doesn't eliminate the entitlement?
Recognizes the edge case: supervisor unavailability makes compliance with the pre-approval rule impossible
Considers whether the policy accounts for emergency or unavoidable overtime situations
Identifies that the policy as written could be interpreted either way — the manager's denial is defensible but not necessarily correct

levels.label: Exceptional
levels.description: Comprehensive, well-structured, identifies non-obvious issues/steps. Shows systems thinking.

levels.label: Strong
levels.description: Covers key points with good structure. May miss one subtle element but demonstrates clear reasoning.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Identifies obvious issues/steps but lacks depth or misses important considerations.

levels.label: Weak
levels.description: Superficial or partially correct. Shows some reasoning but significant gaps.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Misunderstands the task, provides irrelevant response, or demonstrates no structured thinking.

questions.prompt: Five tasks (T1–T5) need to be completed. Given the dependency rules, what is the earliest position T4 can occupy in the sequence?
questions.context:
Dependencies:
- T1 must be completed before T2.
- T3 must be completed before T4.
- T2 must be completed before T5.
- T1 must be completed before T3.
- T4 has no other dependencies beyond T3.
questions.explanation: T4 requires T3 to be done first. T3 requires T1 to be done first. So the minimum chain is: T1 → T3 → T4. That puts T4 at position 3 at the earliest. The full valid sequence could be: T1, T3, T4, T2, T5 — this satisfies all dependencies. T4 cannot be earlier than position 3 because it has a dependency chain of length 2 before it.
